content:
Cross-entropy grew out of **Shannon's information theory** (1948): expected code length under a mismatched codebook is **H(P, Q) = −Σ p log q**, while optimal coding for P achieves **H(P)**.

**Kullback and Leibler** (1951) related the excess to **KL divergence**, cementing cross-entropy as the bridge between entropy and comparing distributions.

In statistics and ML, **maximum likelihood** for Bernoulli and categorical models was recognized as minimizing cross-entropy between empirical label frequencies and model probabilities.

By the deep learning era, **softmax + cross-entropy** became the default classification head, and **next-token cross-entropy** the standard language modeling objective — the same H(P, Q) formula at scale.

timeline.label: Shannon (1948)
timeline.description: Information theory introduces entropy and expected code length under arbitrary distributions — the conceptual origin of cross-entropy.

timeline.label: Kullback-Leibler (1951)
timeline.description: KL divergence formalizes the excess in H(P, Q) beyond H(P).

timeline.label: ML classification (1990s-2000s)
timeline.description: Logistic regression and neural nets adopt log loss / cross-entropy as the standard link to Bernoulli and categorical likelihoods.

timeline.label: LLM era
timeline.description: Token-level cross-entropy over massive vocabularies drives pretraining and fine-tuning of generative models.
